questions.question: Explain the difference between array-based and linked implementation of stacks. Give one advantage of linked implementation.
keyPoints: Array-based: fixed size, linked: dynamic size
Array-based: may overflow when full, linked: overflow only when memory exhausted
Linked implementation: memory allocated as needed
Advantage: efficient memory utilization, no pre-allocation waste

questions.question: Write the algorithm for PUSH operation in a linked stack. What is its time complexity?
keyPoints: Create new node with data
Set new node's next to current top
Update top to point to new node
Time complexity: O(1) - constant time insertion at head

questions.question: What is stack underflow? How is it detected in a linked stack implementation?
keyPoints: Underflow occurs when attempting to pop from empty stack
In linked stack: checked by verifying if TOP == NULL
Should be handled before pop operation to prevent error
Return appropriate error message or code

questions.question: Explain why two pointers (FRONT and REAR) are maintained in a linked queue implementation.
keyPoints: FRONT pointer: used for dequeue operation (deletion from head)
REAR pointer: used for enqueue operation (insertion at tail)
Both pointers ensure O(1) time for both operations
Without REAR pointer, enqueue would require O(n) traversal

questions.question: Describe the steps involved in ENQUEUE operation in a linked queue when it is empty.
keyPoints: Create a new node with the data
Set new node's next pointer to NULL
Both FRONT and REAR pointers point to new node
This is the first and only element in the queue

questions.question: What is memory leak in the context of linked stacks? How can it be prevented?
keyPoints: Memory leak: allocated memory not returned to system after use
Occurs when pop/dequeue does not free the node memory
Prevention: use free() in C or delete in C++/Java after removing node
Proper deallocation prevents memory exhaustion

questions.question: Compare the time complexity of push and pop operations in linked stack with array-based stack.
keyPoints: Linked: both push and pop are O(1)
Array: both push and pop are O(1)
Key difference: array may overflow, linked only when memory exhausted
Linked requires dynamic memory allocation overhead

questions.question: Write a complete C program to implement a linked stack with push, pop, and display operations. Include necessary structure definition and main function to demonstrate the operations.
keyPoints: Define struct node with data and next pointer
Implement push: create node, link to top, update top
Implement pop: check underflow, get data, move top to next, free node
Implement display: traverse from top, print each node's data
Include menu-driven main with switch case for operations
Handle empty stack condition in pop and display

questions.question: Write a complete C program to implement a linked queue with enqueue, dequeue, and display operations. Explain why maintaining both FRONT and REAR pointers is essential for efficiency.
keyPoints: Define node structure for queue
Initialize FRONT and REAR to NULL (empty queue)
Enqueue: create node, if empty set both pointers, else link rear to new node
Dequeue: check underflow, get front data, move front to next, free node, if empty set rear to NULL
Display: traverse from front to rear
Efficiency: O(1) for both operations due to two pointers - without rear, enqueue would be O(n)

questions.question: Compare and contrast linked stacks and linked queues. Explain with examples how both follow different access patterns and provide the time complexity analysis for all basic operations.
keyPoints: Stack: LIFO (Last In First Out), operations at one end (TOP)
Queue: FIFO (First In First Out), insert at REAR, delete at FRONT
Stack push/pop: O(1)
Queue enqueue/dequeue: O(1)
Stack example: function call stack, expression evaluation
Queue example: CPU task scheduling, print queue
Both use dynamic memory, no overflow until memory exhausted
Both require pointer manipulation

questions.question: Explain the concept of dynamic memory allocation in the context of linked data structures. How does it solve the limitations of static (array-based) implementations? What are the additional overheads?
keyPoints: Dynamic allocation: memory requested at runtime using malloc/new
Solves fixed size limitation of arrays
No pre-allocation needed - memory used as required
Solves overflow problem - only limited by system memory
No memory waste from unused allocated space
Overheads: extra memory for pointers (2-8 bytes per node)
Overheads: allocation/deallocation time, potential fragmentation
Trade-off: slightly slower access compared to direct array indexing
